text: The hysterectomy is completed, and the surgeon prepares to begin the pelvic lymph node dissection.
The surgeon holds the harmonic scalpel in their right hand, preparing to use it for the pelvic lymph node dissection.
The surgeon uses a Babcock's forceps to gently grasp the vessels and lymph nodes, ensuring they are held securely without causing damage or disruption.
Two bowel holding graspers are visible on the assisting ports, positioned to stabilize the bowel during the surgical procedure.
The surgeon makes the first surgical incision, a peritoneal cut, parallel and upwards along the lateral border of the external iliac artery.
The external iliac artery is clearly visible in the surgical field, positioned prominently beneath the camera view.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to expose the psoas muscle, revealing its location and structure beneath the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon exposes the psoas muscle, revealing the genitofemoral nerve adjacent to it.
The surgeon moves upwards with strokes, revealing a vein in front of the surgical field.
The deep circumflex iliac vein is visible in the surgical field, positioned near the previously exposed external iliac artery.
The surgeon marks the anatomical structure on the screen with two blue lines, indicating a specific area of interest.
A large lymph node is visible above the marked limit, appearing prominent in the surgical field.
The surgeon indicates that the visible lymph node does not require removal, marking its location in relation to the deep circumflex iliac vein.
The surgeon indicates the upper limit of the surgical field with two blue lines visible on the screen.
The surgeon's instrument curves downwards towards the ilio obturator fossa, following the path of the vein.
The ultrasonic scalpel is used to dissect around the medial border of the external iliac vein, demonstrating its effectiveness as a precise surgical tool.
The surgeon navigates into the ilioobturator fossa, identifying the superior ramus of the pubic bone, also referred to as the pectineal ligament, as a key anatomical landmark.
The surgeon reveals the superior ramus of the pubic bone, which appears as a wide, glistening window reflecting light, indicating successful dissection.

The surgeon identifies and prepares to remove the group of lymph nodes surrounding the external iliac vessels, including nodes adjacent to the external iliac artery and vein. The inter iliac nodes, located between the artery and vein, are also targeted for removal.
The surgeon uses the ultrasonic scalpel to develop the external iliac vessels from their posterior aspect, carefully dissecting around the medial border of the external iliac vein.
The surgeon gently clears the anterior wall of both the external iliac vessels, revealing the underlying structures.
The surgeon holds the fibro-fatty nodal tissue firmly and gently breaks off its connections, visibly separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon exposes the base of the external iliac vessels, revealing the anatomical structures at the junction of the external iliac artery and vein.
The surgeon moves the harmonic scalpel to continue the dissection around the external iliac vessels, focusing on clearing the anterior wall and developing the posterior aspect.
The surgeon clears the posterior aspect of the major external iliac vessels, carefully separating surrounding tissue from the vessels.
The surgeon develops the posterior face of the external iliac vessels, carefully manipulating the surrounding tissue to expose the vessel surface.
The surgeon identifies the iliolumbar space, located between the major external iliac vessels and the psoas muscle.
The external iliac artery and vein are visibly lifted away from the psoas muscle bed, creating a clear separation between these vessels and the underlying muscle.
The surgeon demonstrates the clearance of the base of the ilio obturator zone, highlighting the inadequate fat layer visible unless the external iliac vessels are lifted adequately.

The surgeon identifies the bifurcation of the external iliac vein and the internal iliac vein, lifting the vessels to facilitate dissection. The ilio obturator space is developed, revealing a large vein in the surgical field.
The aberrant obturator vein is visible, draining directly into the external iliac vein.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the aberrant obturator vein as it becomes more distinct within the surgical field.
The obturator nerve is visible emerging between the bifurcation of the external iliac vein and the internal iliac vein.
The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing the internal iliac vein clearly in the surgical field.
The surgeon marks the aberrant obturator vein with blue and the obturator nerve with yellow. The internal iliac vein is identified as a crucial landmark for safe nodal dissection.

The surgeon follows the obturator nerve, identifying a large vein running above it and draining into the external iliac vein. The assistant uses a grasper to guard the vein while the surgeon clears its superior border.
The surgeon holds the aberrant obturator vein with a Babcock's forcep, highlighting its position and manipulation during the dissection.
The vein runs parallel to the obturator nerve, which is visible just below, extending towards the obturator foramen.
The obturator nerve is visible entering the obturator foramen alongside the obturator vein.
The surgeon holds a stout aberrant obturator vein with a Babcock's forcep, visually identifiable as arising from the external iliac vein and moving towards the obturator foramen.

The video illustrates three variations of the aberrant obturator vein: Variation one shows the vein draining into the inferior epigastric vein medial to the femoral ring; variation two shows it draining lateral to the ring; and variation three shows the vein draining directly into the external iliac vein, as observed in this case.
The obturator artery is identified in the surgical field, with no additional notable structures or variations present.
The surgeon proceeds to the right hemipelvis, where the external iliac artery is clearly delineated after the removal of lymph nodes on the left side.
The external iliac vein is visible as the surgeon clears the inter iliac group of lymph nodes, carefully dissecting around the vein to avoid damage.
The surgeon has made a peritoneal incision parallel and upwards to the external iliac artery, preparing to remove the nodal chain from the external iliac artery.
The surgeon uses small, precise movements with the harmonic scalpel to gently dissect tissue, progressing upwards with careful strokes.
The surgeon continues the dissection upwards, revealing the deep circumflex iliac vein as it drains into the external iliac vein.
The deep circumflex iliac vein is observed crossing over the external iliac artery, highlighting the anatomical relationship between these vascular structures.

The surgeon identifies the cranial limit at the bifurcation of the common iliac artery into the external and internal iliac arteries. A node is visible near the deep circumflex iliac vein, which is gently held in place.
The deep circumflex iliac vein is visible crossing over the external iliac artery to enter into the external iliac vein, marking the higher limit of the surgical field.
The surgeon reaches the landmark of the deep circumflex iliac vein, which is visually identifiable in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins developing the medial border of the external iliac vein to gain entry into the ilio obturator fossa, carefully manipulating the surrounding tissue.

In the ilio-obturator fossa, the surgeon identifies the first landmark, which is the bone or the superior ramus, along with the pectineal ligament that glistens and shines. The surgeon then broadens the windows or spaces in this area.
The surgeon applies traction to the obliterated umbilical artery, ensuring it remains visible and accessible during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery as the medial landmark within the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies a large vein in the pelvis, noting the presence of anatomical variation. Attention is drawn to the possibility of this variation being mirrored on the opposite side, potentially identifying it as the aberrant obturator vein.
The surgeon carefully removes lymph nodes located both above and below the external iliac vein, ensuring the vein remains undamaged during the procedure.
The surgeon holds the vein securely while manipulating the lymph node, which is visibly continuous from the upper area towards the lower region.
The surgeon carefully slips the entire nodal mass from behind the identified vein, ensuring the vein remains intact and undamaged during the procedure.
The obturator nerve is now visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon has cleared away the ilio obturator lymph nodes, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The obturator internus muscle is visible, and the obturator nerve is held firmly to facilitate the removal of the nodal mass.
The internal iliac vein is visible peeping from below, positioned just beneath the obturator nerve.
The surgeon identifies the internal iliac vein, allowing for confident manipulation around the obturator nerve to clear the surrounding lymph nodes.
The internal iliac vein is visible, running beneath the obturator nerve in the surgical field.
The surgeon points out the bifurcation of the external and internal iliac venous system, highlighting the anatomical landmark in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes a mass, revealing the bifurcation of the internal and external iliac veins.
The surgeon performs final clearance at the medial border near the bifurcation of the iliac venous system, ensuring complete removal of surrounding tissue.
The surgeon clears the medial border of the external iliac vein, revealing the anatomical structures around the vein.
The internal iliac vein is clearly visible in the surgical field, with the surgeon maintaining a view of the vein at all points.
The surgeon confidently removes the nodal mass, ensuring awareness of the depth and position of the internal iliac vein, which is visible in the surgical field.
The obturator vein is visibly draining directly into the external iliac vein, with the drainage occurring at the obturator foramen.
The final view of the lymph node dissection is displayed, showing the cleared area around the external iliac vein and the medial border near the bifurcation.
The anatomical structures, including the external and internal iliac veins, are clearly visible, with distinct borders and surrounding tissue highlighted.
The surgeon identifies the deep circumflex iliac vein and the inferior epigastric vein, which is positioned above. The aberrant obturator vein is observed joining into the obturator foramen.
The aberrant obturator vessel is visible, joining into the obturator foramen.
The surgeon performs dissection using an ultrasonic scalpel, specifically the harmonic device, without employing any other energy sources.
